title: Underglaze pencil drawing on air-dry clay
subtitle: Drawing fine lines and detail onto unfired clay with underglaze pencils
excerpt: Underglaze pencils are graphite-like sticks of coloured ceramic pigment that draw directly onto bisqueware or unfired clay. On air-dry clay, they act as a drawing tool for adding fine lines and detail that acrylic brushwork cannot match. This technique covers how to use them on air-dry clay, how to fix the marks with varnish, and how to layer them with painted areas.
sourceNotes: Underglaze pencil drawing on unfired clay is a standard studio technique adapted here for the air-dry clay track. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A ceramic colourant applied to clay before a glaze coat. On air-dry clay it is used as a surface colourant sealed with varnish rather than glaze.
Fully air-dried and no longer damp.
Clay that has been fired once, at low temperature, to harden it without glazing. In air-dry clay tutorials, bone-dry serves the same structural role.

content.text: An underglaze pencil is a stick of compressed ceramic pigment mixed with a binder. It draws on clay the way a graphite pencil draws on paper, leaving fine coloured lines that acrylic brushwork cannot replicate. On air-dry clay,
underglaze
pencils are used as a surface-decoration tool sealed with varnish rather than glaze. This technique applies equally to a finished pot, a flat tile, or a decorative plaque.
When to use it
Apply underglaze pencils to
bone-dry

air-dry clay only. The pencil drags on damp clay and smears. The harder, smoother surface of a bone-dry piece accepts the pencil marks cleanly. A sanded surface takes marks more evenly than an unsanded one.
What you need
Underglaze pencils in chosen colours. A set of six covers most decorating needs. Available from ceramic suppliers.
Bone-dry air-dry clay piece, sanded smooth
Fine sandpaper (180 grit)
Clear acrylic varnish for sealing
Soft brush for removing dust before drawing
Step-by-step
Sand the surface of the bone-dry piece with fine sandpaper to create a smooth, even texture. Wipe away all dust with a soft brush or barely damp cloth.
Sharpen the underglaze pencil to a fine point using a sharp craft knife or a ceramic pencil sharpener. A blunt pencil drags and produces uneven marks.

Sketch the design lightly on paper first. Then draw directly onto the clay surface with the pencil, using light but consistent pressure. Underglaze pencil marks can be removed from bone-dry clay by sanding and redrawing, which gives some scope for correction.
Build up lines in multiple passes rather than pressing hard in one pass. This gives cleaner edges and allows for hatching and tonal variation.

To combine pencil work with painted areas, apply the painted acrylic background first and allow it to dry fully before drawing over the top with the pencil. The pencil marks over a painted surface are less absorbent, so the lines sit on top rather than sinking in.

Once drawing is complete, apply two coats of clear acrylic varnish to seal the pencil marks permanently. Apply the varnish with a soft brush and avoid scrubbing; the pencil marks can smear if the brush passes over them repeatedly while the first varnish coat is still wet.
What it looks like when it is working
Clean, fine lines sit on the clay surface with the same precision as pencil lines on paper. The marks have a slightly powdery appearance before sealing. A well-drawn
bisque
-quality surface accepts the pencil at light pressure without tearing or dragging. After varnishing, the lines deepen in colour and gain clarity.
Common mistakes
Drawing on damp clay: the pencil drags and tears the surface. Wait for full bone-dry.
Pressing too hard: the pencil digs into the clay and the marks are wide and rough. Use light, multiple passes instead.
Sealing with a wet brush: scrubbing the first varnish coat smears pencil marks. Apply with a soft brush in one direction only.
Skipping the sanding step: an unsanded surface has uneven texture. The pencil lines will vary in thickness and look rough.
